taskProfile.historicalSimilarity: DOM-004 (content-security domain overlap, prior security model documentation)

patterns.title: High invariant count correlates with first-attempt success for update tasks
patterns.insight: First-attempt tasks applied more invariants on average (13.1 vs 6.7 in DOC-3137). The 3 highest invariant counts (T-008: 24, T-007: 18, T-012: 29) all achieved first-attempt acceptance. Encourage writers to explicitly apply more invariants upfront.
patterns.applicability: Directly applicable — this task updates existing pages (gap-fill). Writers should apply invariants comprehensively for the permission requirement sections being added.

patterns.title: Only 5.2% of invariants caused rejections. The invariant inventory is well-calib
patterns.insight: Only 5.2% of invariants cause rejections. Top rejection causes are formatting/structural rules (callout titles, dash types, frontmatter), not content/accuracy rules. Formatting invariants need stronger pre-writing emphasis.
patterns.applicability: Applicable — permission documentation will include callouts and structured formatting. Writers should prioritize formatting invariant compliance.

patterns.title: Focused single-persona developer update pages achieve first-attempt acceptance
patterns.insight: Single-persona developer update pages with medium complexity achieve high first-attempt success (100% in DOC-3137). Profile: action=update, contentType=howto, targetPersonas=[developer].
patterns.applicability: Partially applicable — developer pages (Page permission management, Workflows) match this profile; the business-user page (Edit and publish pages) requires extra care per DOM-008.

patterns.title: Cross-reference update tasks achieve near-perfect first-attempt rates
patterns.insight: Cross-reference update tasks achieve 75% first-attempt rate (vs 56% overall). With INV-structure-017, cross-ref content accuracy hits 100% on first attempt.
patterns.applicability: Applicable — this task requires cross-reference insertions between 4 pages. These are low-risk and can be batched or deprioritized for review overhead.

patterns.title: Code-sample mechanical conversion with INV-codesamples-* suite + build verification achieves first-attempt acceptance
patterns.insight: Full INV-codesamples-* suite with build verification achieves 100% first-attempt acceptance for mechanical code-sample transformations. Only works for compilable files.
patterns.applicability: Low applicability — this task is primarily prose documentation about permissions, not code sample transformation.

patterns.title: Minimal-scope code-only updates with 2-4 invariants achieve first-attempt acceptance
patterns.insight: Narrow-scope tasks (≤2 code blocks, 2-4 invariants) consistently pass first-attempt review. Lowest-complexity tier, essentially risk-free.
patterns.applicability: Low direct applicability — this task involves prose updates. However, the cross-reference insertion sub-tasks are narrow-scope and should achieve first-attempt success.

antiPatterns.title: Create-action pages have higher rejection rates than updates
antiPatterns.insight: Create tasks averaged 3.33 attempts vs update 1.33 in DOC-3137. Create tasks trigger frontmatter, cross-ref, and structural rejections, consuming 40% of writer attempts despite being 19% of tasks.
antiPatterns.applicability: Applicable — the task adds new sections analogous to create-within-update. Writers need explicit guidance on structural conventions and cross-ref identifiers for new content.

antiPatterns.title: When acceptance criteria say 'document methods of interface X' without enumerati
antiPatterns.insight: Acceptance criteria saying 'document X' without explicit enumeration lead to missed items. Code analysis should feed explicit checklists into task planning.
antiPatterns.applicability: Applicable — the task defines 4 operations with specific per-operation requirements. The task planner should enumerate each operation's exact permission requirements as acceptance criteria.

antiPatterns.title: Style invariant INV-formatting-016 (em dash vs en dash) is the most frequent ...
antiPatterns.insight: Writers consistently produce em dashes (—) when the style guide requires en dashes (–). INV-formatting-016 is the most frequent rejection trigger across all runs (19% of tasks in DOC-3137).
antiPatterns.applicability: Applicable — the permission sections will contain list items and descriptive text where dash usage is common. Writers should be explicitly reminded of the em-dash/en-dash rule.

antiPatterns.title: Writers trust pre-existing code examples without verifying API surface, propagating phantom method calls
antiPatterns.insight: Writers trust pre-existing code examples without verifying API surface, propagating phantom method calls. Documentation .md code blocks bypass build verification.
antiPatterns.applicability: Low applicability — this task adds permission requirement prose sections, not code examples.

domainInsights.title: Multi-layer parameter architectures require per-layer disambiguation in documentation
domainInsights.insight: APIs with layered architectures create disambiguation challenges when concepts at different layers share terminology. Documentation must explicitly map each concept to its layer.
domainInsights.applicability: Highly applicable — the page permission model is explicitly multi-layered (application-level → ACL → workflow). Each layer has its own permission concepts that share terminology. Documentation must disambiguate these layers clearly.

domainInsights.title: Hub/index pages require manual enumeration updates when new pages are created
domainInsights.insight: Xperience uses manual hub/index pages enumerating sub-pages in prose. When new content is added, parent index pages must be updated manually.
domainInsights.applicability: Applicable — the role-management parent page may need enumeration updates if Page permission management page structure significantly changes.

domainInsights.title: Xperience documentation spans two content collections: _documentation (reference
domainInsights.insight: Xperience documentation spans _documentation and _guides collections. Feature changes affecting authentication and content security impact both collections.
domainInsights.applicability: Directly applicable — page permissions are an authentication/content-security feature. The task explicitly limits scope to _documentation; _guides work should be noted as deferred.

domainInsights.title: Xperience API reference pages follow a dual-page pattern: a conceptual how-to pa
domainInsights.insight: Xperience API reference follows a dual-page pattern: conceptual how-to + reference table. Impact mapper must capture both pages when a feature area changes.
domainInsights.applicability: Moderately applicable — page permissions are documented on a management page (conceptual) and referenced from how-to pages. Dual-page awareness helps ensure all affected pages are updated.

domainInsights.title: Xperience business-user documentation requires domain-specific interaction verb
domainInsights.insight: Business-user pages require domain-specific interaction verbs (INV-style-034, INV-style-036) and face unique accuracy challenges around UI behavior. Stale icon references cause rejections.
domainInsights.applicability: Highly applicable — 'Edit and publish pages' is a business-user page. Permission cross-references added there must use approved interaction verbs and current UI element references.

domainInsights.title: Pre-existing documentation code examples contain phantom API method calls invisible to compilation
domainInsights.insight: Documentation code blocks in .md files can contain phantom API method calls that persist indefinitely. Only accuracy review catches these since .md code is not compiled.
domainInsights.applicability: Low applicability — primarily relevant to code example updates, not this permission documentation task.

sourceObservations.title: Base-class property with high internal fanout predicts dedicated documentation section need
sourceObservations.insight: A base-class property consumed at 7+ internal call sites needs a dedicated documentation section, not just a table cell. High internal fanout predicts multi-page documentation impact.
sourceObservations.applicability: Principle applies: each permission type (Read, Update, Create, Delete) should get its own documented section rather than a single table, given their cross-cutting impact across operations.

sourceObservations.title: Shared lexical root on different type hierarchies predicts disambiguation section need
sourceObservations.insight: Two concepts sharing a lexical root but on different type hierarchies need explicit disambiguation. Without it, developers assume they are interchangeable.
sourceObservations.applicability: Applicable — 'View' appears at application-permission level (SystemPermissions.VIEW) and could be confused with page-level display/read. 'Update' in ACL could be confused with workflow step transitions.

sourceObservations.title: API methods with 3+ similar-type parameters predict named parameter documentation need
sourceObservations.insight: API methods with 3+ similar-type parameters need named parameter syntax in code examples to disambiguate call semantics.
sourceObservations.applicability: Low applicability — this task is primarily prose documentation about permissions, not code sample updates.

styleEvolutions.title: Incorrect page_link identifiers triggered rejections in 2 tasks (TASK-002, TASK-
styleEvolutions.insight: Writers fabricate page_link identifiers instead of verifying against actual front matter. Cross-reference accuracy requires identifier verification against target page frontmatter.
styleEvolutions.applicability: Highly applicable — this task requires cross-references between 4 pages. All page_link identifiers must be verified against actual frontmatter, not guessed.
